title: Kundalini and Humility: What the Awakening Process Teaches About Ego
meta_description: Hey, you. Yeah, you, staring at your screen in the dark, heart pounding, maybe a strange buzzing in your head or a fire in your gut. I know that feeling. I
body:
Kundalini and Humility: What the Awakening Process Teaches About Ego

Hey, you. Yeah, you, staring at your screen in the dark, heart pounding, maybe a strange buzzing in your head or a fire in your gut. I know that feeling. I've been there. It's 3 AM, and you're wondering if you're losing your mind or if something truly wild is happening. You're not alone. What you're probably wrestling with is what people call "spiritual awakening" - often, specifically, kundalini awakening. And let me tell you, it's not all rainbows and unicorns like some of those Instagram gurus make it out to be. It's raw. It's terrifying. And it absolutely shreds your ego to pieces, whether you like it or not.

I'm Kalesh. I've walked through that fire. I've felt the terror, the confusion, the isolation. I've had moments where I genuinely thought I wouldn't make it. But here's the thing: you can. And what you learn about yourself, about reality, about that stubborn little thing called ego - it changes everything. It doesn't just change you; it rebuilds you. And that rebuilding starts with a whole lot of demolition.

So yeah, let's talk about kundalini and humility. Because you can't have one without the other, not really. This isn't about some airy-fairy concept; it's about what happens when your entire world gets turned inside out.

The Brutal Awakening: Why Your Ego Hates Kundalini

You know that sense of self you've built up over years? Your identity, your achievements, your opinions, your control? That's your ego. It's what tells you who you are, what you're good at, what you deserve. It's the little manager in your head, organizing everything, keeping you safe (or so it thinks). When kundalini starts to stir, it's like a cosmic wrecking ball smashing into that meticulously constructed edifice. It doesn't ask for permission. It doesn't care about your carefully planned life. It just is.

I remember lying in bed, feeling this intense heat, this electric current moving through my body, and my mind just couldn't make sense of it. My ego was screaming. "What is this? You're sick! You're going crazy! Stop it!" But you can't stop it. That's the first lesson in humility: you're not in charge. Not of this. Not anymore. This force is bigger than you, bigger than your understanding, bigger than anything you've ever encountered. And it's going to show you just how small your perceived control actually is.

You'll experience sensations you can't explain. Visions, sounds, emotional rollercoasters that make no sense. Your body might twitch, shake, or feel like it's burning. You might have periods of intense bliss followed by agonizing despair. Your ego, which thrives on predictability and order, will be in utter disarray. It's like someone pulled the rug out from under your entire existence, and now you're flailing in the dark. Right?

This is where many people panic. They run to doctors, therapists, religious leaders - all looking for an explanation, a diagnosis, a way to "fix" it. And often, those well-meaning people don't have the language or the experience to understand what's happening. You're told it's anxiety, psychosis, depression. And while those things can certainly be triggered or exacerbated by the process, they're not the root cause. The root cause is a spiritual unfolding, and your ego is fighting it tooth and nail because it perceives it as a threat to its very existence. Which, in a way, it is. The old you is dying. And that's terrifying.

I've read a lot of books trying to make sense of my own experience, and I'd recommend some of them. For a good overview of what this process can look like, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) was a lifesaver for me because it validated so many of the bizarre things I was going through. It showed me I wasn't alone, and that others had similar symptoms. That alone is a huge relief when you're feeling isolated.

The Stripping Away: Losing Your Sense of Self

One of the hardest parts of this whole thing is the feeling of losing yourself. Your identity, the labels you've clung to - parent, professional, artist, intellectual, spiritual seeker - they all start to feel flimsy. What you thought was important suddenly isn't. Your values shift. Your interests change. Friends you once connected with deeply might feel alien. Your ego built itself on these external validations and roles, and now they're dissolving.

You might find yourself questioning everything: your career, your relationships, your beliefs. This isn't just a mid-life crisis; it's a soul crisis. You're being forced to confront the illusion of who you thought you were. And that's where humility truly kicks in. You realize how little you actually know, how little control you actually have, and how much of your "self" was just a construct, a story you told yourself and others.

I remember looking at my reflection and not recognizing the person staring back. It wasn't a physical change; it was an internal one. The confidence, the certainty I once had - gone. Replaced by a deep sense of not knowing, of being utterly vulnerable. My ego, which loved to be "right" and "in control," was getting its ass handed to it. And honestly, it sucked. It was painful. It was disorienting.

But here's the secret: that disorientation is the beginning of something new. It's the fertile ground where genuine humility can grow. You're not losing yourself; you're losing the false self. You're shedding layers that no longer serve you, layers that were built on fear and societal conditioning. It's like a snake shedding its skin - it's uncomfortable, but it's necessary for growth.

This process of shedding can make you feel extremely exposed. You might find yourself crying for no apparent reason, or feeling intense anger that seems to come out of nowhere. These are old emotions, old wounds, stored within your body and psyche, being brought to the surface for release. Your ego wants to suppress them, to keep them hidden, because it sees them as weaknesses. But the awakening process forces you to face them, to feel them, and to let them go. That's true courage, and it's born out of humility - the willingness to be utterly vulnerable and imperfect.

If you're going through this, and you feel like you're losing your grip, know that you're not alone. Books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can provide a lot of comfort and practical advice during these intense periods. Greenwell really gets into the nitty-gritty of what happens when your spiritual engine revs up unexpectedly.

The Great Equalizer: Seeing Through the Illusion

Once your ego starts to crack, you begin to see things differently. The world doesn't look the same. People don't look the same. You start to see beyond the masks, beyond the roles, beyond the superficial. You see the underlying unity, the interconnectedness of everything. And that's a truly humbling experience.

You realize that all the things you thought made you special - your intelligence, your beauty, your wealth, your spiritual achievements - are just temporary constructs. They're not who you truly are. What you truly are is something far vaster, far more deep, and utterly indistinguishable from everything else. That's the ultimate ego death - the realization that you are not separate. You are part of the whole. And in that realization, all sense of individual superiority or inferiority dissolves.

I remember a moment, after weeks of intense internal turmoil, where I felt this incredible wave of love and connection wash over me. It wasn't a personal love; it was universal. And in that moment, all my worries, all my self-importance, all my fears, just vanished. I saw the divine in everything, in everyone. And I saw it in myself, not as a special, individual me, but as the same consciousness that animated everything else. Know what I mean? It's like the universe whispers a secret in your ear: "You're not special, and neither is anyone else. We're all just expressions of the same thing." And that's incredibly freeing.

This realization strips away the need to compare yourself to others, to compete, to prove your worth. Because if we're all at its core the same, what is there to prove? What is there to compare? Humility becomes your natural state, not because you're belittling yourself, but because you've seen through the illusion of separation. You understand that true power doesn't come from dominating others or accumulating things; it comes from aligning with that universal flow, from serving something bigger than your small self.

The Practice of Humility: Living Post-Awakening

So, you've been through the wringer. Your ego's been hammered. You've had glimpses of something far beyond your wildest dreams. Now what? The real work begins: living it. Humility isn't a one-time event; it's a continuous practice. Because your ego, that sneaky little devil, it's always trying to rebuild itself. It'll try to claim credit for your insights, it'll try to make you feel special because you've had an "awakening," it'll try to create a new, "enlightened" identity. Don't let it.

Living with humility means:

  • Acknowledging you don't know everything: Even after deep experiences, there's always more to learn, more to understand. The moment you think you've "arrived" is the moment your ego starts to puff up again.
  • Serving others without expectation: True humility isn't about self-abasement; it's about recognizing your connection to all beings and wanting to contribute positively without needing applause or recognition.
  • Being open to correction: If someone points out a flaw or a blind spot, listen. Your ego wants to defend itself, but humility allows you to consider other perspectives and grow.
  • Embracing imperfection: You're human. You'll make mistakes. You'll have off days. Humility means accepting that, forgiving yourself, and moving on without dwelling in self-criticism or self-importance.
  • Releasing the need for control: Surrender becomes a way of life. You do your best, but you let go of the outcome, trusting that something larger is at play.

I'll tell you what, after my crisis, I had to learn to live with a completely different operating system. My old ways of thinking, reacting, and interacting just didn't work anymore. I had to learn to listen to my body, to my intuition, rather than just my analytical mind. It was a slow, sometimes painful process of re-calibration. And every time I thought I had it figured out, something else would come along to humble me again. That's just how it works. It's a constant shedding, a constant refining.

For those times when you feel like you're losing your way again, or you need some guidance on the practicalities of dealing with intense energetic shifts, The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) offers some solid ground. It's not just theory; it's got real advice for managing the physical and psychological aspects of this journey.

Beyond the Crisis: The Gift of True Self

Look, I know it feels like a crisis right now. And it is. But it's also an opportunity. A brutal, terrifying opportunity to become who you were always meant to be. The ego, for all its faults, serves a purpose. It helps you deal with the physical world, it gives you a sense of individuality. But it's not the whole story. It's not your true self. The awakening process forces you to confront that distinction.

When you come out the other side - and you will, I promise you - you'll find a new sense of self. One that's not built on external validation or fragile constructs, but on a deep, abiding connection to something far greater. You'll find a quiet confidence that doesn't need to brag or dominate. You'll find a genuine compassion that comes from recognizing yourself in others. And you'll find a deep sense of peace that transcends circumstances.

This isn't about becoming a spiritual superstar. It's about becoming genuinely human. It's about shedding the pretense and embracing your true nature, which is by nature humble, interconnected, and loving. Does that land? Because that's the real prize here. Not superpowers, not enlightenment in the conventional sense, but a return to your authentic self, free from the shackles of ego.

So, when you're feeling overwhelmed, when you're questioning your sanity, remember this: you're not alone. This is a journey many have taken, and many are taking right now. It's messy. It's hard. But it's worth it. Allow yourself to be humbled. Allow yourself to be broken open. Because that's where the light gets in. That's where you find the real you.

If you're still feeling lost in the woods, or wondering if what you're experiencing is truly spiritual or something else entirely, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) offers a broader context for unusual states of consciousness. It helped me understand that my experience, while intense, wasn't unique, and fit into a larger pattern of human spiritual emergence. It can help you feel less isolated and more understood.

Hold on. Breathe. You've got this. The universe is just giving you a very intense, very personal lesson in humility. And it's going to make you stronger, wiser, and more genuinely you than you've ever been before.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
